title.zh: Permutation Parity and Twisty Puzzles Solving (4)

content.zh:
Permutation Parity and Twisty Puzzles Solving (4)

Yujian Song

This tutorial is the continuation of the previous three tutorial Permutation Parity and Twisty Puzzles Solving. I am going to list some way to generate basic formulas (3-cycles, two-pair exchanging formulas etc.).

We have discussed two widely used ways: commutators and conjugate. Let’s have a simple review.

Commutator

Supposing A and B represent two permutations, if there is only one common element affected by these two permutations together, the combination of permutations [A, B]=A B A' B' becomes a 3-cycle of three elements.

If A and B affect more than one element (including the element 1) in common, we can know that A B A' B'=(1, B'(1), A'(1)), in which A'(1) (or B'(1)) represents the element moved to the position 1 by A (or B).

To explain how to use the formula above on puzzles, here is a example of Pyraminx:

In the image above, if wanting do the 3-cycle (1, 2, 3) for the numbered edge blocks, which is block 1 to position 2, block 2 to position 3, and block 3 to position 1, we just need to find a formula A satisfying the equation A'(1)=3, which means that the formula A can make the block on position 3 be moved to position 1, then find another formula B which can make the new block on the position 2 be moved to the position 1, and let A and B have only one common position (the position 1). It is easy to find that the condition is satisfied when A=L and B=R', so A B A' B'=L R' L' R is a suitable 3-cycle, which is exactly a commonly used formula in Pyraminx layer-by-layer method.

Sometimes, when using commutators to generate 3-cycles, there might be some steps being able to cancel each others. For example, one of the corner 3-cycle formulas in blindfolded-solving of 3×3×3 Cube is [R' D R U' R D' R', U'], and it is actually a step-canceled formula from the original formula [R' D R U' R D' R' U, U'], in which the two part formulas affect one common element only.

Of course, not all 3-cycles are commutators, and a simplest example is the edge 3-cycle formula in 3×3×3 Cube that R U’ R U R U R U’ R’ U’ R2. But anyway, it is very convenient and effective to use commutators to generate 3-cycles, and commutators always play a important role in solving puzzles or blindfolded-solving.

Conjugate

Conjugate is the combination of setup and reverse. It is usually happening that finding a suitable 3-cycle for any three blocks by using commutators is very inconvenient. So we need to do a setup (formula B) to move the blocks we want to solve to suitable positions, where the three blocks can be exchanged by a known 3-cycle (formula A) easily, then do the reserve of setup (formula B’), and the process above is the 3-cycle of any three positions. The formula B A B' can be simply recorded as [B: A].

Of course, A does not have to be a 3-cycle, but can be any formula.

Two-pair Exchanging & 5-cycle

We have known that if there is only one common element affected by A and B together, the commutator A B A’ B’ is a 3-cycle. What if there are two common elements? Using the same process as having one common element, we can get the prove below.

If the two permutations A and B affect more than three elements, and the common elements is 1 and 2, we can prove that:

  1. In A and B, if 1 and 2 are neighboring in both two permutations, the commutator A B A’ B’ is a two-pair exchanging. In addition:
  2. If the directions of 1 and 2 are the same (for example, both are moving 1 to position 2) in two permutations, A B A' B'=(1, 2)(A'(1), B'(1));

  1. If the directions of 1 and 2 are different (for example, one is moving 1 to position 2 and another is opposite) in two permutations, A B A' B'=(1, A'(1))(2, B'(2)).

  1. If 1 and 2 are neighboring in permutation A (supposing moving 1 to position 2) but not neighboring in permutation B, A B A' B'=(1, 2, B'(2), B'(1), A'(1)), which is a 5-cycle.

  1. If 1 and 2 are not neighboring in both two permutations, A B A' B'=(1, B'(1), A'(1))(2, B'(2), A'(2)), which are two disjoint 3-cycles.

The word “not neighboring” in the states above means not only in the same cyclic permutation, but also in different cyclic permutations. Here are some examples:

So what is the use of two-pair exchanging and 5-cycles? They can be used to build up 3-cycles. When simple 3-cycles are hard to find, it is another method to generate 3-cycles.

Using two-pair exchanging to build up 3-cycles:

Using 5-cycles to build up 3-cycles:

Using 5-cycles to build up 3-cycles can play an important role when solving some special puzzles.

Period

Now we know some way to generate two-pair exchanging or 5-cycles. But in fact, the preconditions of these methods are very demanding for many puzzles. For example, in common 3×3×3 Cube, ignoring the direction of blocks, doing R U' R' U can make two-pair exchanging for corner blocks, but at the same time, edge blocks are also taken a 3-cycle. So how to do corner two-pair exchanging only, and keep edge blocks unchanged?

Generally, if formula A does different operations for elements of different clusters in a puzzle, but we want to separate only one operation for a specific cluster, how should we do it?

One method that might be useful is to repeatedly use A to act on puzzles.

We have discussed that, if the structure of a puzzle is a group, all formulas is permutations of its blocks, which means using the same formula repeatedly to act on the puzzle will definitely make the puzzle return to the starting state. Because different clusters do not affect each other, when the periods of clusters are different, using formula A repeatedly can make some clusters return to the starting state after several cycles, but other clusters do not return to the starting state after these cycles, therefore different permutations are able to be separated.

Suppose formula A = ab1b2...bk, where a,b1,b2,...,bk means permutations in different clusters, then suppose that the order (period, in other words) of a is m, and the order of b1,b2,...,bk is n1,n2,...nk. These permutations follow the Multiplication Commutative Law. If m is relatively prime to every ni (i = 1,2,...,k), m is relatively prime to the product of all ni (i = 1,2,...,k), therefore . So we can use formula to separate the permutation of the first cluster out.

For R U' R' U in 3×3×3 Cube, this formula affect two clusters: corner cluster is two-pair exchanging, and edge cluster is a 3-cycle. So if making it self-multiply three times, the new formula A3=(R U' R' U)3 is two-pair exchanging for corner blocks only.

Using periods to separate permutations usually need to act on puzzles for many times. For example, only doing R U' R' U for 15 times on FTO can get the corner two-pair exchanging formula.

(R U' R' U)15

Of course, anyway, using periods to separate permutations is always worth to have a try. As for the states which cannot use period method, such as the permutations having the same period, we will discuss next time.

At last, here is a Megaminx formula using conjugate, two-pair exchanging and periods methods:

y L' (R U2 R' U') (R U R' U')2 (R U' R') L

This Megaminx PLL formula can be seen as y [L' R U: (U R' U' R)3]. Do periodic U R' U' R to generate the corner two-pair exchanging formula firstly, then use conjugate to move these two pair blocks to specific positions.

The software used in this tutorial, GAP, is a totally free computing algebraic system. For details, please visit this website: http://www.gap-system.org/.

algs: y L' (R U2 R' U') (R U R' U')2 (R U' R') L
